name: services
context: Expose Kubernetes workloads with Services — ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, and headless for service discovery
info: Services provide stable networking for ephemeral Pods. A Service selects Pods by label and provides a stable DNS name and virtual IP that persists across Pod restarts, rescheduling, and scaling. Agents use Services to expose applications within the cluster (ClusterIP), on node ports (NodePort), via cloud load balancers (LoadBalancer), or as DNS-only discovery (headless). This skill covers Service types, selectors, ports, DNS resolution, and Endpoints. For HTTP routing and TLS, see [[kubernetes/ingress]].
triggers: how do services work in kubernetes
body:
# Services

A Service is a stable networking abstraction over a set of [[kubernetes/pods]]. Pods are ephemeral — they get new IPs when recreated. Services provide a fixed DNS name and virtual IP (ClusterIP) that routes traffic to matching Pods via label selectors.

## Service Types

| Type | Access | Use Case |
|------|--------|----------|
| `ClusterIP` | Internal only | Default. Microservice-to-microservice communication |
| `NodePort` | External via node IP:port | Dev/test, direct node access |
| `LoadBalancer` | External via cloud LB | Production external traffic (AWS ALB/NLB, GCP LB) |
| `ExternalName` | DNS CNAME alias | Map to external DNS (e.g., RDS endpoint) |
| Headless (`clusterIP: None`) | DNS only, no proxy | StatefulSets, direct Pod discovery |

## ClusterIP Service (default)

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-app
  namespace: production
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: web-app
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 80
      targetPort: 8080
      protocol: TCP
```

Other Pods access this via `http://web-app.production.svc.cluster.local` or just `http://web-app` from the same namespace.

## NodePort Service

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-app-nodeport
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: web-app
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8080
      nodePort: 30080  # optional: auto-assigned from 30000-32767 if omitted
```

Access via `http://<any-node-ip>:30080`.

## LoadBalancer Service

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-app-lb
  annotations:
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: nlb  # AWS-specific
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: web-app
  ports:
    - port: 443
      targetPort: 8080
      protocol: TCP
```

The cloud provider provisions a load balancer and assigns an external IP. Check with `kubectl get svc web-app-lb` — the `EXTERNAL-IP` column shows the LB address.

## ExternalName Service

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: external-db
spec:
  type: ExternalName
  externalName: mydb.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com
```

Creates a DNS CNAME record. Pods accessing `external-db` are redirected to the external hostname.

## Headless Service

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: my-stateful-app
spec:
  clusterIP: None
  selector:
    app: my-stateful-app
  ports:
    - port: 5432
```

No ClusterIP is allocated. DNS returns individual Pod IPs. Used with StatefulSets for direct Pod addressing (`pod-0.my-stateful-app.namespace.svc.cluster.local`).

## kubectl Service Commands

```bash
# Create service imperatively
kubectl expose deployment web-app --port=80 --target-port=8080 --type=ClusterIP

# List services
kubectl get services -n production
kubectl get svc -o wide

# Describe (shows endpoints, selector, events)
kubectl describe svc web-app

# View endpoints (Pod IPs backing the service)
kubectl get endpoints web-app

# Quick test from inside cluster
kubectl run curl-test --rm -it --image=curlimages/curl -- curl http://web-app.production.svc.cluster.local

# Port-forward a service locally
kubectl port-forward svc/web-app 8080:80

# Delete
kubectl delete svc web-app
```

## DNS Resolution

Kubernetes DNS (CoreDNS) creates records for every Service:

| Format | Example |
|--------|----------|
| `<service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local` | `web-app.production.svc.cluster.local` |
| `<service>.<namespace>` | `web-app.production` |
| `<service>` (same namespace) | `web-app` |

SRV records are created for named ports: `_http._tcp.web-app.production.svc.cluster.local`.

## Multi-Port Services

```yaml
spec:
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 80
      targetPort: 8080
    - name: metrics
      port: 9090
      targetPort: 9090
```

When a Service has multiple ports, each port **must** have a `name`.

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `KUBECONFIG` | Path to kubeconfig file |
| `KUBE_NAMESPACE` | Default namespace |

sections.title: Service types
sections.body: Four types: **ClusterIP** (default) — internal-only virtual IP. **NodePort** — exposes on every node at a static port (30000-32767). **LoadBalancer** — provisions a cloud load balancer with an external IP. **ExternalName** — CNAME alias to an external DNS name. Plus **headless** (clusterIP: None) — no proxy, DNS returns Pod IPs directly. Choose ClusterIP for internal microservices, LoadBalancer for production external traffic, NodePort for development, headless for StatefulSets.

sections.title: Selectors and endpoints
sections.body: Services select Pods via `spec.selector` (label matching). The Endpoints controller maintains an Endpoints object listing the IPs of all matching, Ready Pods. When no Pods match the selector, the Service has no endpoints and traffic goes nowhere. Debug with `kubectl get endpoints <service>`. For services without selectors, manually create Endpoints objects to point to external IPs.

sections.title: DNS and service discovery
sections.body: CoreDNS creates A/AAAA records for every Service: `<svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local`. Within the same namespace, just use `<svc>`. SRV records are created for named ports. Headless services return Pod IPs instead of the ClusterIP. DNS-based discovery means services find each other by name without hardcoded IPs. Environment variables (SERVICE_HOST, SERVICE_PORT) are also injected but DNS is preferred.
